title: CODE OF PRACTICE FOR THE PREVENTION AND REDUCTION OF MYCOTOXINS IN SPICES

sections.section: CODE OF PRACTICE FOR THE PREVENTION AND REDUCTION OF MYCOTOXINS IN SPICES
sections.summary: Document title: Code of Practice for the Prevention and Reduction of Mycotoxins in Spices.
Identifies the Codex document code CXC 78-2017 and states it was adopted in 2017.

Introduces that spice production, processing, packaging and distribution are complex, may involve many establishments and long timeframes, and commonly include cleaning, grading, drying and sometimes grinding or microbial‑mitigation treatments across multiple locations.

States the objective: establish a general code of practice to prevent and reduce mycotoxins in spices to as low as reasonably achievable by applying GAP, GMP and GSP throughout the food chain to reduce consumer exposure.
Defines the Code's scope as applying to spices in whole, broken, ground or blended form and explicitly excludes dried aromatic herbs.

Advises using this Code alongside the Code of Hygienic Practice for Low Moisture Foods (CXC 75‑2015) and other relevant Codex codes, and describes the Code as a recommended approach producers, transporters, processors and manufacturers should follow as far as practicable, allowing flexibility in primary production if equivalent control measures ensure product safety.

Defines 'spices' as dried plants or plant parts (roots, rhizomes, bulbs, bark, flowers, fruits, seeds) used for flavouring, colouring or aroma in whole, broken, ground or blended forms, and defines 'source plant' as the non‑dried plant from which the spice is derived.

sections.section: 2. RECOMMENDED PRACTICES BASED ON GOOD AGRICULTURAL PRACTICES (GAP), GOOD MANUFACTURING PRACTICES (GMP) AND GOOD STORAGE PRACTICES (GSP)
sections.summary: Section heading introducing recommended practices based on Good Agricultural Practices (GAP), Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP) and Good Storage Practices (GSP), covering pre‑harvest, post‑harvest, drying, transport and storage measures.

Recommends GAP to reduce toxigenic fungi, including crop rotation and avoiding proximity to known host crops, reducing plant stress via irrigation/fertilization/pruning and pest/disease control, minimizing insect damage through IPM and registered insecticides, controlling weeds and debris, using disinfected seeds, careful fungicide use, avoiding untreated organic waste, avoiding spray irrigation during flowering, choosing well‑drained soils, and removing diseased or injured plants.

sections.section: 2.1.1 Spices  are  susceptible  to  contamination  by  toxigenic  fungi  in  the  field,  during  drying  and  storage.
sections.summary: Declares spices are susceptible to contamination by toxigenic fungi in the field, during drying and storage, and reiterates recommended GAP measures such as crop rotation, stress reduction, IPM, seed disinfection, cautious fungicide use, avoiding untreated organic waste, avoiding spray irrigation during flowering, ensuring good drainage and removing diseased plant material.

Specifies post‑harvest measures: measure moisture at harvest and avoid harvesting high‑moisture crops or mechanically damaged material; begin drying immediately using thin layers, frequent turning, protection from rewetting, and monitoring to limit time in the critical water activity range (aw 0.80–0.95) — five days or less in the drying yard is recommended and a target aw ≤0.65 is generally protective. It also requires clean, dry transport containers for undried commodities to processing, and storage measures to process fresh material quickly, use porous packaging stored off the floor and away from walls, control insects/rodents and moisture (example storage 5–8 °C, RH <75%), and clean/disinfect facilities with appropriate substances.

Requires moisture determination for each harvested load and avoidance of harvesting at high moisture (e.g., after rain or dew) because of prolonged drying and increased fungal/mycotoxin risk; mandates avoidance of mechanical damage, removal or separate handling and cleaning/drying/evaluation of mouldy or ground-fallen material (except rhizomes), use of clean plastic under plants where feasible, differential harvesting of ripe crops, harvesting only amounts that can be processed timely, observance of fungicide pre-harvest intervals, and documentation of harvest measurements and deviations.

Stipulates immediate on-farm drying after harvest on appropriate, contaminant-free surfaces with thin layers (typically 3–5 cm), frequent turning, night ventilation, protection from animals/insects and rewetting, lot segregation and monitoring (including instrumental moisture checks and worker training), use of suitable drying surfaces and equipment maintenance; notes three drying stages by water activity and warns that aflatoxin/OTA-producing fungi grow at aw 0.80–0.95 (so control time in that range), recommends five days or less in the risky aw range and a target aw ≤0.65 for protection.

Requires that containers and transport equipment be clean, dry and free of residues, insects and visible fungal growth, that commodities not dried to a safe storage moisture be transported without delay to a processing plant for drying, and that transport allow aeration and minimize condensation while protecting the cargo from rain.

Advises hygienic transport practices for spices per CXC 75-2015 Annex III, including placing bags on pallets, using fully ventilated containers where possible, employing desiccants (e.g., calcium chloride), promptly cleaning spillages, maintaining clearance from container roofs and using saddle stow to maximize airflow and minimize bag damage.

Requires rapid packaging of hygroscopic dried spices in moisture-barrier materials (vacuum/MAP options noted) after they have been dried to safe moisture levels, using new bags for field packing, applying sanitary practices equivalent to establishment packing where feasible, and using packaging to prevent insect access and moisture ingress.

Requires labelling of best-before dates and storage instructions per the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1-1985) based on studies of packaging and shelf conditions, and recommends consumer storage guidance (cool, dry, ventilated area away from heat and humidity, avoid refrigeration to prevent condensation) and use/care tips to prevent mould growth.
